accounts.name: Sainsbury's
accounts.quick_context: CA In Motion — NDA sent 8 Jun, deadline 19 Jun. 12m interactions/yr across multiple brands. Key use case: predictive CSAT across 100% of interactions.
accounts.group_structure: Sainsbury's plc — UK's second-largest supermarket. Includes Argos, Tu Clothing, Nectar. Owned by institutional shareholders post-Qatar Investment Authority exit.
accounts.financials: Revenue ~£29bn FY2025. Cost efficiency programme active. AI/tech budget significant given Microsoft 5-year partnership.
accounts.champions: Kieran McLoughlin (Group Head of Product, Contact Centre)
accounts.blockers: NDA not yet signed. Clodagh Moriarty (CRTO / economic buyer) not yet directly engaged. Microsoft 5-year strategic partnership is deepest competitive risk.
accounts.competitors: Microsoft (5-year strategic partnership), Genesys (incumbent CCaaS with native AI overlays), Concentrix (outsourced agent model)
accounts.why_cresta_wins: Insights/QM layer above existing stack — not displacing Genesys. Woolworths (identical stack) is killer reference. ROI hook: supply chain contact driver (product quality tied to supplier decisions).
accounts.cc_footprint: 12m interactions annually across multiple brands. Concentrix as BPO partner. ~87% of customer sentiment invisible post-interaction. ACW and QM gaps identified.
accounts.regulatory_signals: Ofgem-equivalent retail regulation applies. No direct FCA exposure but significant data governance requirements given scale.
accounts.narrative: Sainsbury's has 12m annual interactions across multiple brands, yet 87% of customer sentiment is invisible post-interaction. Their Microsoft 5-year AI partnership and new AI Centre of Excellence signal readiness — but Copilot alone won't solve contact centre insight gaps. Cresta as the intelligence layer above Genesys/Concentrix gives them predictive CSAT, supply chain contact drivers, and QM at scale without disrupting their existing stack.
accounts.outreach_angle: Predictive CSAT across 100% of interactions — vs CX Index sampling. Supply chain contact driver (product quality calls tied directly to supplier decisions).
use_cases: Predictive CSAT across 100% of interactions (vs CX Index sampling)
accounts.best_case_study: Woolworths (retail — 12m+ interactions, identical Genesys stack, contact driver insight); TSB (financial services QM at scale)

accounts.name: Simply Business
accounts.quick_context: IDD Complete — deal paused to 23 Sept at Bea Montoya's request due to competing supplier launch. CA proposed for Northampton office.
accounts.group_structure: Simply Business — UK's largest business insurance broker (SME focus). US operations via Boston office. B Corp certified. Owned by Travelers Insurance (US).
accounts.financials: ~£200m GWP. Travelers-owned — budget decisions may involve US approval. Cost efficiency focus alongside tech investment.
accounts.blockers: Competing RPA/automation supplier being onboarded (identity unknown). Buy vs build risk from Josh Dawson (Head of Data Science) and US CTO. Timing/bandwidth constraint until September.
accounts.competitors: Competing RPA/automation supplier (identity unknown). Internal build risk via Josh Dawson.
accounts.why_cresta_wins: IDD complete — architecture and rollout already mapped. Bea is genuinely bought in, not sceptical. Cresta is not competing with RPA — it's the insight layer above voice/chat stack.
accounts.cc_footprint: UK: 7.8m annual voice minutes, 72k chat, 275k email. 132 FTE agents (renewals/service). AHT 267s, ACW 159s. Automation rates: portal 50%, chat 18-20%, email 0.5%, voice 0.2%.
accounts.regulatory_signals: FCA regulated — data sensitivity is gating CA. Consumer Duty obligations require full interaction monitoring. Data sensitivity around policyholder calls.
accounts.narrative: Simply Business has the clearest path to value of any account in the territory — IDD complete, architecture mapped, Bea (COO) fully bought in. The pause is bandwidth, not scepticism. The CA data will prove value within weeks. Snap Finance is the closest reference: Insights changed programme priorities in weeks, not quarters.
accounts.outreach_angle: Consumer Duty compliance requires 100% interaction monitoring — Cresta is the only way to get there on Twilio/Zendesk without ripping out the stack.
use_cases: ACW reduction (159s baseline — significant agent time saving)
Contact driver insight across 7.8m voice minutes
Agent Assist on Twilio voice channel
accounts.best_case_study: Snap Finance (Insights changed programme priorities in first weeks); TSB (insurance/financial services Agent Assist + QM)

accounts.name: Hyperoptic
accounts.quick_context: In-person meeting 29 June 2-3pm at Hyperoptic Hammersmith. Colin proactively shared operational stats for business case. Commercial proposal being built.
accounts.group_structure: Hyperoptic — UK's largest full-fibre broadband provider (FTTP). Private — backed by Mubadala Investment Company. Fast growth trajectory.
accounts.financials: Private. Revenue estimated £100m+. Subscriber growth driving investment in customer service infrastructure.
accounts.blockers: Commercial structure TBC — pricing proposal in progress. Competitive landscape unknown.
accounts.competitors: Unknown — likely reviewing multiple AI vendors given telecom sector AI activity.
accounts.why_cresta_wins: Colin is self-driven and proactively sharing data — buying intent is clear. Hyperoptic's FTTP growth creates direct scaling pressure on the care team. Vodafone and Sky are closest telecoms references.
accounts.cc_footprint: Care contact centre handling voice calls and messages. Exact agent count from Colin's operational stats (shared 17 Jun). Fast subscriber growth driving contact volume growth.
accounts.regulatory_signals: Ofcom regulated. Consumer vulnerability obligations. FTTP installation contacts are high-volume, time-sensitive.
accounts.narrative: Hyperoptic is the fastest-growing FTTP provider in the UK — subscriber growth is outpacing their care infrastructure. Colin has self-qualified by proactively sharing operational stats for a business case. The 29 June in-person meeting is a pivotal moment to present the commercial proposal and close on next steps.
accounts.outreach_angle: FTTP subscriber growth is creating a care scaling crisis — Cresta provides the insight layer to understand contact drivers and automate where possible without ripping out their stack.
use_cases: Contact driver insight (installation-related contacts at scale)
accounts.best_case_study: Vodafone (telecoms); Sky (CCaaS integration, Agent Assist at scale)

accounts.name: JD Sports
accounts.quick_context: Pre-RFP stage — Woolworths reference call self-organising between Neil Bradford and Foad Safari (APAC). 3-year AI transformation project forming.
accounts.group_structure: JD Sports Fashion plc — global sports fashion retailer. 3,400+ stores worldwide. Listed on FTSE 100. Multiple sub-brands including Footasylum, Size?, Blacks.
accounts.financials: Revenue ~£10bn FY2025. EBITDA under pressure from US tariffs and macro headwinds. Cost efficiency programme active.
accounts.blockers: Retail case study gap for Cresta (being addressed via Woolworths reference). No response to free AI Agent POC offer. Volume data (annual minutes, WISMO %) not yet received from Neil.
accounts.competitors: Multiple AI vendors actively demoing — Neil confirmed highest demo volume in any business area. SmartAgent (incumbent CCaaS) may have native AI overlay.
accounts.why_cresta_wins: Woolworths reference directly addresses retail gap. WISMO use case (35-40% of care volume) is a clear ROI hook. Neil is running reference calls proactively — buying intent is clear.
accounts.cc_footprint: UK customer care via SmartAgent. Ascensos as BPO partner. WISMO (where-is-my-order) estimated at 35-40% of care volume. US business already 100% AI-first for first contact.
accounts.regulatory_signals: GDPR. Consumer Rights Act. No specific FCA/Ofgem exposure.
accounts.narrative: JD Sports has 35,000 employees, 3,400+ stores, and a US business that's already 100% AI-first for customer contact. The UK is next — Neil Bradford is building a 3-year AI transformation programme. WISMO is 35-40% of care volume and is the beachhead use case. Woolworths is the reference that closes the retail credibility gap.
accounts.outreach_angle: Your US business is already 100% AI-first. The UK programme is forming now — Cresta has the retail proof points to anchor the business case.
use_cases: WISMO contact elimination (35-40% of care volume)
Agent Assist for UK customer care
accounts.best_case_study: Woolworths (retail — direct reference being arranged); CarMax (retail automotive CX); Brinks Home (QM at scale)

accounts.name: Lowell Group
accounts.quick_context: Demo proposal sent 9 Jun after call with Dan Snell and Mark Hayward. Two key use cases scoped: portfolio anomaly detection and new book onboarding. Awaiting response.
accounts.group_structure: Lowell Group — pan-European debt purchaser and collections company. UK HQ in Leeds. Operations across UK, Germany, Nordics, Austria.
accounts.financials: ~£500m revenue. Debt purchasing and collections at scale. Cost efficiency and recovery rate optimisation are core business drivers.
accounts.blockers: No response to demo proposal yet (sent 9 Jun). Verint incumbent — relationship exists but gaps acknowledged by Dan.
accounts.competitors: Verint (incumbent WFM/QM — relationship exists but gaps acknowledged). Collections-specific AI players.
accounts.why_cresta_wins: US Bank (collections) is a direct, credible reference. Verint gaps explicitly flagged by Dan on the 29 May call — Cresta as complement, not replacement. Portfolio anomaly detection is a differentiated use case.
accounts.cc_footprint: Large collections contact centre across UK and Europe. Verint deployed for WFM/QM. Specific gaps: portfolio anomaly detection, new book onboarding, contact driver analysis.
accounts.regulatory_signals: FCA regulated (collections). Consumer Duty obligations. Strict requirements around vulnerability handling and call recording.
accounts.narrative: Lowell has a Verint installation with acknowledged gaps — Dan named portfolio anomaly detection and new book onboarding as priorities on the 29 May call. US Bank (collections) is the direct proof point. The demo proposal maps directly to these two use cases. This is a fast-start opportunity if the demo lands well.
accounts.outreach_angle: Your Verint setup has gaps — portfolio anomaly detection and new book onboarding aren't solved by legacy QM tools. Cresta Insights answers both, with US Bank as direct proof.
use_cases: Contact driver analysis across collections calls
accounts.best_case_study: US Bank (collections — directly referenced in proposal); Avant (credit/collections); Brinks Home (QM at scale)

accounts.name: UK Power Networks
accounts.quick_context: Paused at Alex Williams' request — 'We liked you guys so we will certainly reach out.' Re-engage Sept/Oct. Three clear use cases: misdirected Monday contacts, ACW, Ofgem QM.
accounts.key_quote: We liked you guys so we will certainly reach out.
accounts.group_structure: UK Power Networks — electricity distribution network operator (DNO) for London, South East and East of England. Owned by Hong Kong-based Cheung Kong Infrastructure.
accounts.financials: ~£1.5bn regulated revenue. Regulated by Ofgem under RIIO-ED2 framework. Technology investment driven by regulatory obligations.
accounts.champions: Alex Williams (Head of Customer Contact Centre)
accounts.blockers: Multiple competing projects. Alex proactively paused — bandwidth, not scepticism. Content Guru is incumbent CCaaS.
accounts.competitors: Content Guru (named incumbent in Salesforce data).
accounts.why_cresta_wins: Alex explicitly positive ('We liked you guys'). Three use cases with direct regulatory hooks: Ofgem QM compliance, storm-day contact insight, ACW reduction. No competitive process underway.
accounts.cc_footprint: Customer contact centre handling storm/outage calls, vulnerability contacts, general enquiries. Post-call ACW explicitly named as top priority. Misdirected Monday contacts (storm-related) are a recurring cost.
accounts.regulatory_signals: Ofgem RIIO-ED2 regulated. Consumer vulnerability requirements mandatory. QM for vulnerable customer calls is a compliance need, not just efficiency.
accounts.narrative: Alex is warm, has explicitly said they'll reach out, and has three use cases with direct Ofgem compliance hooks. Storm-day contact driver insight is a unique angle — Alaska Airlines is the best analogy. The October re-engagement should lead with the QM/vulnerability compliance angle, not efficiency.
accounts.outreach_angle: Ofgem Consumer Vulnerability obligations mean you need 100% QM on vulnerable customer calls — Cresta delivers this without changing your Content Guru stack.
use_cases: Ofgem-compliant QM for vulnerable customer calls
Storm-day contact driver insight (misdirected Monday contacts)
accounts.best_case_study: United Airlines (contact driver elimination — 6m calls/yr); Alaska Airlines (storm-day analogy); Brinks Home (QM at scale with NPS uplift)
